planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or direct sow in fall for spring germination; Maltese cross self-sows freely and can become a weed in favorable conditions — deadhead before seeds mature to limit spread. It is short-lived (2–4 years) but self-sows to maintain colonies. Plants in poor soil stay compact; rich soil produces tall, floppy plants that may need staking.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Maltese cross is a classic cottage-garden perennial from Russia and Siberia producing one of the most intensely saturated and vivid scarlet reds available in early-summer perennials — upright, bristly-hairy stems topped with flat-topped, dense clusters of small flowers, each petal notched into a distinctive cross shape that gives each floret a Maltese cross outline, and in the aggregate creating round, vivid-scarlet flower heads of high visual impact. The scarlet is hot, clear, and uncompromising — a true scarlet without orange or pink dilution. This saturated color, in combination with the flat-topped, dense cluster form, creates one of the most eye-catching early-summer effects in the mixed border. It is a traditional cottage garden plant with centuries of cultivation. It combines dramatically with blue delphiniums or white Shasta daisies in the classic red-white-blue color schemes of traditional English cottage gardens. Self-sows freely to maintain colonies; the white form 'Alba' is also available. Tends to be short-lived but perpetuates itself by seed.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; red campion is dioecious (separate male and female plants) — sow generous quantities so that both sexes are represented. It self-sows prolifically and can naturalize readily in hedgerows, woodland edges, and semi-shaded garden areas. Plants live 2–4 years but self-sow continuously.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to woodland edge; red campion is a widespread European wildflower of woodland edges, hedgerows, and shaded banks — among the most common and cheerful May-flowering wildflowers of the British countryside, producing branched stems of deeply rose-pink, five-petaled, notched flowers from May through September in a cheerful, informal, prolific display that weaves among other wildflowers and garden plants with naturalizing ease. It is dioecious — male and female flowers occur on separate plants, the males slightly smaller and more numerous — and freely hybridizes with the white campion (Silene latifolia) where both occur, producing the intermediate hybrid Silene x hampeana with pale-pink flowers. A classic companion to bluebells and cow parsley in the British spring and early-summer hedgerow. In the garden it naturalizes beautifully in semi-shaded borders, woodland-edge gardens, and cottage-garden plantings, self-sowing to maintain colonies without invasive behavior. Double-flowered forms ('Flore Pleno') with pompom-like blooms are available and particularly ornamental.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in fall; unlike most hybrid tulips, Tulipa sylvestris reliably perennializes and spreads vegetatively by stolons — it does not need lifting and replanting annually. It tolerates partial shade and naturalizes well under deciduous trees and in grass. Allow foliage to die back naturally after blooming to build bulb reserves.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; wild tulip is a species tulip that dramatically differs from the large hybrid garden tulips in its ability to reliably naturalize and return year after year — spreading vegetatively by underground stolons to form expanding colonies that flower more profusely over time rather than declining as do most hybrid tulips. The flowers are smaller and more delicate than hybrid tulips, elegantly star-shaped when fully open, in clear, warm, soft yellow with a faint greenish flush on the outer petals — and they have a pleasant, sweet fragrance absent from most tulips. Native across a vast range from the Mediterranean to central Asia, it has naturalized in historical European orchards and vineyards where it was planted during the Renaissance. Outstanding for naturalizing in grass under deciduous trees, in orchard lawns, and in rock gardens where its modest size and naturalizing habit suit semi-wild plantings. One of the few tulips that succeeds in zone 9 because it does not require deep cold to perform.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or fall or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; Himalayan bergenia is a smaller, more compact species than the commonly grown B. cordifolia and better suited to small gardens or tight spaces. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become congested and flower production decreases.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; Himalayan bergenia is a compact species from the mountainous regions of Afghanistan, Kashmir, and the Himalayas — smaller in leaf and overall stature than the familiar elephant ears bergenia (B. cordifolia) but sharing its essential character of thick, rounded, leathery, evergreen leaves that develop rich burgundy-red tones in winter cold. The early-spring flowers — appearing in March and April from pink-red flower stems that may even push through snow — are held in neat, rounded clusters of pure white to pale-pink, cup-shaped blooms, each sweetly fragrant (unlike B. cordifolia). The combination of compact size, fragrant white flowers, good winter color, and unusual origin from extreme Himalayan altitudes gives this species a distinctly different quality from the coarser B. cordifolia. A better choice for small shade gardens, containers, and woodland-edge situations where B. cordifolia would be too large. The cultivar 'Bach' is a hybrid derived in part from B. stracheyi with outstanding winter foliage color.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in cool weather — the tiny seeds need light to germinate and should not be covered; diamond flower blooms within 4–6 weeks of germination and self-sows freely to maintain naturalized colonies in mild climates. Sow in succession every 3–4 weeks for extended bloom. In mild-winter areas (zones 8–10) sow in fall for winter and early spring flowering.

directions: Surface-sow seeds in cool weather in moderately fertile, moist, well-drained soil in sun to part shade; diamond flower is a Portuguese native miniature annual that is one of the most charming and overlooked small-scale plants in cool-season horticulture — a diminutive, delicate plant only 2–3 inches tall producing tiny, round leaves on thread-fine stems and, from the earliest spring, a constant succession of four-petaled flowers only 1/4 inch across in pale lilac to nearly white with a faint violet vein that gives the flowers a crystalline, diamond-like delicacy when viewed closely. The effect of a large mass is a soft, hazy, low carpet of pale lilac that looks from a distance like a lilac-colored mist at ground level. It self-sows freely between stepping stones, in crevices in paving, at the base of walls, and in any shaded position where the soil stays moist in spring. Outstanding for filling cracks between paving stones and at the base of containers, where its self-sowing habit creates naturalized colonies that need no further effort. The individual plants die in heat but the self-sown seedlings carry the colony through several seasons.
